payload.message: We’ve got the smoking-ish gun: earlier sessions listed `synology-monitor tool_count=5`; after the restart, the current session only shows “waiting … startup_complete=false” and never reaches “listed tools” for Synology. I’m checking the exact log window around that failed startup now.

content.text: We’ve got the smoking-ish gun: earlier sessions listed `synology-monitor tool_count=5`; after the restart, the current session only shows “waiting … startup_complete=false” and never reaches “listed tools” for Synology. I’m checking the exact log window around that failed startup now.

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"@'
import sqlite3
path = r'C:\Users\ahazan2\.codex\logs_2.sqlite'
con = sqlite3.connect(f'file:{path}?mode=ro', uri=True)
cur = con.cursor()
rows = cur.execute("select id, ts, level, target, substr(feedback_log_body,1,2200) from logs where id between 1143600 and 1144480 order by id").fetchall()
for id, ts, level, target, body in rows:
    b = body or ''
    if any(s in b.lower() for s in ['synology', 'nas-mcp', 'mcp-remote', '502', 'bad gateway', 'failed', 'error', 'stderr', 'server_name=', 'streamable']):
        print('--- id', id, 'ts', ts, level, target)
        print(b.replace('\n',' ')[:2200])
con.close()
'@ | python -","workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\Codex\2026-04-30\we-are-auditing-serious-performance-and-2","timeout_ms":20000}

Use `nodeRepl.write(text)` when you want exact text output in the tool result; it writes the string exactly as given and does not append a newline. Prefer it over `console.log(...)` for final output, JSON, or other text you plan to consume programmatically. `console.log(...)` is still useful for ad hoc debugging or object inspection because it formats values and appends line breaks automatically. Use `await nodeRepl.emitImage(imageLike)` to return images; each call adds one image to the outer tool result, so call it multiple times to emit multiple images. Supported image inputs are a data URL, inferred PNG/JPEG/WebP bytes, or `{ bytes, mimeType }`. Saved references to `nodeRepl.write(...)` and `nodeRepl.emitImage(...)` stay reusable across calls, but async callbacks that fire after a call finishes still fail because no exec is active. Top-level bindings persist across calls until `js_reset`. If a call throws, prior bindings remain available and bindings that finished initializing before the throw often remain reusable. For reusable names that may be assigned again later, prefer top-level `var name = ...`; `var` can be redeclared across calls. If you hit `SyntaxError: Identifier 'x' has already been declared`,

tools.name: automation_update
tools.description: Create, update, view, or delete recurring automations in the Codex app. Use this when the user asks for an automation, recurring run, repeated task, reminder, follow-up, monitor, or asks you to watch something, keep an eye on it, check back later, wake up later, notify them, or keep working later. Cron automations run as standalone jobs against workspaces. Heartbeat automations are proactive follow-ups attached to the current local thread. Prefer heartbeats for requests to continue this thread later, especially below one hour. Never write raw automation directives by hand, show raw RRULE strings to the user, or create a workaround cron automation for a thread heartbeat unless the user explicitly asks for that. For requests about existing automations, inspect $CODEX_HOME/automations/*/automation.toml to find matching automation ids by name or prompt. Prefer updating an existing automation over creating a duplicate. For updates, preserve existing fields unless the user asks to change them, and call automation_update with the resolved id and full updated fields.

cwds.description: Cron automations only. Workspace directories for the automation; can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.

destination.description: Optional automation destination. Use thread for heartbeat automations attached to the current local thread.

executionEnvironment.description: One of worktree or local. Cron automations only.

id.description: Automation id. Required for mode=view, mode=update, mode=delete, and mode=suggested_update. Omit for mode=create and mode=suggested_create.

kind.description: One of cron or heartbeat. Required for create, update, suggested_create, and suggested_update. Use cron for detached workspace jobs. Use heartbeat when the user wants this thread to wake up later and continue the conversation.

localEnvironmentConfigPath.description: Optional local environment config path for worktree setup scripts. Omit to preserve the existing selection on update, or pass null to clear it. Cron automations only.

mode.description: One of view, create, update, delete, suggested_create, or suggested_update. Use view to show an existing automation, create/update/delete to mutate immediately, and suggested_create/suggested_update to present a proposal for the user to review.

model.description: Model to use for cron automations.

name.description: Short human-readable automation name. If the user does not provide one, choose a concise name.

prompt.description: The automation prompt. Describe only the task itself; do not include schedule, workspace, or thread details because those are provided separately. Keep it self-sufficient, include output expectations when useful, and do not ask it to write a file or announce nothing to do unless the user explicitly asked for that.

reasoningEffort.description: Reasoning effort to use for cron automations. One of none, minimal, low, medium, high, or xhigh.

rrule.description: RRULE schedule string. Interpret requested times in the user's locale. Cron automations use hourly interval or weekly schedules. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can use minute-based intervals such as FREQ=MINUTELY;INTERVAL=30 or daily/weekly wall-clock schedules.

status.description: One of ACTIVE or PAUSED. Default to ACTIVE unless the user asks to start paused.

targetThreadId.description: Target thread id for heartbeat automations. Prefer destination=thread for the current local thread instead of inventing or copying raw thread ids.

payload.message:
The local Codex config looks correct: `synology-monitor` is enabled and points at `https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp`.

The failure is remote-side, not local registration. Codex tried to start it, but `mcp-remote` hit:

```text
Connecting to remote server: https://nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp
Connection error: Streamable HTTP error: Error POSTing to endpoint: Bad Gateway
Fatal error ... code: 502
```

That explains why I still do not see the Synology tools. Earlier logs show it used to load successfully with `tool_count=5`, but after restart it stays at `startup_complete=false` and never lists tools.

What to fix next:

1. Check/restart the service behind `nas-mcp.designflow.app`, not Codex.
2. Check the Cloudflare/hosting route for `nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp`; `502 Bad Gateway` usually means the edge reached the route but the Worker/origin/upstream failed.
3. Once that endpoint returns normally, restart/refresh Codex MCP servers and the tools should appear.
4. Important: local Codex logs include MCP stderr lines with bearer tokens from `mcp-remote`; redact those logs before sharing, and consider rotating the Synology MCP token after debugging.
